name: projects
context: Create, list, inspect, and update GitLab projects with REST API calls that work on GitLab.com and self-managed hosts
info: This skill covers the project lifecycle in GitLab: listing projects visible to the authenticated principal, retrieving project metadata by numeric ID or URL-encoded path, creating new projects in a personal namespace or group namespace, and updating operational settings like visibility, topics, branch defaults, and feature access levels. Agents use it to bootstrap repositories, discover the correct project identifier before downstream API calls, align project settings with policy, and inspect whether issues, merge requests, or pipelines are enabled.
triggers: look up a gitlab project by path
body:
# GitLab Projects

Projects are the anchor object for nearly every other GitLab API workflow. Once an agent knows the project ID or full path, it can branch into issues, merge requests, pipelines, variables, branches, and repository settings.

```bash
# List projects visible to the current token
python ./scripts/manage-projects.py list --search platform --membership

# Create a new project with an initialized README
python ./scripts/manage-projects.py create my-service \
  --namespace-id 12345 \
  --description 'Service owned by the platform team' \
  --visibility private \
  --initialize-with-readme
```

GitLab's Projects API accepts either a numeric project ID or a URL-encoded path such as `group%2Fproject`. The script in this node accepts the human form (`group/project`) and encodes it automatically. Use [[gitlab/auth]] before running it and branch into [[gitlab/issues]], [[gitlab/merge-requests]], or [[gitlab/pipelines]] after the project is known.

sections.title: List and inspect projects
sections.body: `GET /projects` lists projects visible to the current principal and supports filters like `membership`, `owned`, `search`, `visibility`, and `updated_after`. `GET /projects/:id` returns the full project object and accepts either a numeric ID or a URL-encoded path. Listing supports offset pagination with `page` and `per_page`, and large project inventories can move to keyset pagination on the API side.

sections.title: Create projects safely
sections.body: `POST /projects` creates a project in the authenticated user's namespace unless `namespace_id` points at a group or subgroup. `default_branch` can be set at creation time only when `initialize_with_readme=true`. `initialize_with_readme` and `import_url` are mutually exclusive, and visibility is one of `private`, `internal`, or `public`.

sections.title: Update settings and feature access
sections.body: `PUT /projects/:id` updates settings such as `description`, `topics`, `visibility`, `issues_access_level`, `merge_requests_access_level`, `builds_access_level`, `remove_source_branch_after_merge`, and merge policy settings like `only_allow_merge_if_pipeline_succeeds`. This is the right endpoint when an agent must align a project with team conventions before opening merge requests or triggering deployments.

sections.title: Project IDs versus full paths
sections.body: GitLab calls the project selector `id`, but it accepts either the numeric project ID or a URL-encoded path such as `group%2Fsubgroup%2Fservice`. Agents often start with a human path from a ticket or repository checkout, so it is common to resolve a project with `group/project` first and then reuse the numeric ID from the response for later workflows.

links.context: Select the correct token type and host before calling the Projects API
Project management starts with the right token header and scope
Merge requests are scoped to a project
Project settings often need alignment before an agent opens a merge request
Pipelines are triggered and inspected per project
CI/CD variables are stored at the project level
Project environments often need scoped variables after creation
Project URLs are used as Git remotes once the repository exists

related.note: Projects API calls need an authenticated principal with sufficient role on the namespace
A newly created project is commonly followed by remote setup or cloning
Once a project exists, the next workflow is usually a branch push and MR creation
GitHub repository management is the closest equivalent on another platform

scripts.name: Manage GitLab Projects
scripts.description: List, inspect, create, and update GitLab projects via the REST API

GITLAB_TOKEN.description: Preferred API token; use unless OAUTH_TOKEN or CI_JOB_TOKEN is provided instead

OAUTH_TOKEN.description: Optional OAuth bearer token for delegated access

CI_JOB_TOKEN.description: Optional CI job token for in-pipeline automation

GITLAB_HOST.description: GitLab host, including scheme, for example https://gitlab.example.com

GITLAB_HTTP_DEBUG.description: Set to 1 to print request details to stderr

project.description: Numeric project ID or full path like group/project

namespaceId.description: Namespace or group ID for project creation

depends.reason: The correct token type and host must be established before project API calls succeed

author: dojo-community
